headline: Artemis II Mission Sets New Deep-Space Distance Record, Pushing Boundaries for Lunar Exploration
deck: The crewed test flight—carrying four astronauts aboard the Orion capsule—surpassed previous human deep-space records, validating critical systems for future lunar and Martian missions.
body: Artemis II astronauts have become the first humans to travel beyond low Earth orbit since the Apollo 17 mission in 1972, marking a significant milestone in humanity's return to deep-space exploration. The mission, launched on April 2, is pushing the boundaries of human spaceflight and validating critical technologies for future lunar and Martian endeavors.

The Orion capsule, carrying astronauts Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen, lifted off at 6:35 pm ET aboard a Space Launch System rocket. This crew represents a diverse team leading NASA's ambitious program to reestablish a human presence on the Moon.

As of Thursday, April 4, the mission has already set a new record for human distance from Earth. The Artemis II astronauts will travel approximately 10,300 kilometers beyond the moon, significantly surpassing all previous records.

The previous record for distance from Earth was held by the Apollo 13 mission, which reached approximately 400 kilometers beyond the moon. Artemis II's trajectory demonstrates a substantial leap in deep-space capability and endurance.

The mission employs a complex figure-eight trajectory, designed to use a gravitational slingshot during its flyby to return to Earth. The spacecraft will not enter lunar orbit, instead leveraging the moon's gravity for its return journey.

During this flyby, Artemis II will make its closest approach to the lunar surface at 7,400 kilometers. This will occur as the capsule passes the far side of the moon, a critical phase of the mission's intricate path.

A primary objective of Artemis II is to rigorously test technological systems vital for subsequent deep-space missions. This includes evaluating radiation shielding capabilities and ensuring robust communication between the capsule and Earth at lunar distances.

The mission's orbit is specifically optimized for a passive reentry, a crucial safety feature. This design ensures the capsule can return safely to Earth even in the unlikely event of engine failure, highlighting advanced engineering for crew protection.

Reentry is scheduled for April 11, concluding a journey of 9 days and 13 hours after launch. The Orion capsule will ditch in the Pacific Ocean, where it will be recovered by the US Navy, bringing the crew home after their historic voyage.

Artemis II serves as a crucial crewed test flight in NASA's broader program to return humans to the Moon, validating the systems and procedures necessary for future lunar missions, including a lunar landing. The mission's success is a vital precursor to sustained lunar presence and eventual human missions to Mars.
